distractor_rationales: This adds the products instead of subtracting; the determinant is \( ad - bc \).
This is the negative of the determinant (the diagonals were swapped).
This multiplies down the wrong pairs of entries.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{bmatrix} \). Expanding along the first row, what is \( \det(A) \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses all plus signs in the cofactor expansion instead of the alternating \( +,-,+ \) pattern.
This uses all minus signs after the first term instead of the alternating pattern.
This drops the middle cofactor term entirely.
This is the 'rule of Sarrus' diagonal-product sum with the second triple of diagonals omitted.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: For a (upper) triangular matrix, the determinant is just the product of the diagonal entries: \( \det(A) = ([[a]])([[d]])([[f]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This sums the diagonal entries (that would be the trace), not their product.
This incorrectly adds a contribution from the off-diagonal entries \( b,e \), which do not affect the determinant here.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies entries from the first row instead of the main diagonal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
